content.text: Slip marbling creates a flowing, veined pattern on flat clay surfaces by dragging two contrasting colours of liquid
slip

into each other with a needle tool or comb before they dry. The result resembles stone marbling and works on tiles, slabs, and flat-based forms. It is one of the oldest surface-decoration techniques in ceramics and translates directly onto air-dry clay without any kiln.
When to use it
Apply marbled slip to a freshly rolled slab while the clay is still soft, or to a surface that has reached the
leather-hard
stage and has been dampened lightly so the base coat will bond. Avoid applying slip to
bone-dry
clay: the slip will not adhere and will flake off as it contracts on drying. The technique is best suited to horizontal surfaces where the slip can pool and stay put while you drag through it.
What you need
Step by step
Roll the clay to an even 8 mm thickness on a wooden board. Cut your tile or slab shape with the fettling knife. Leave the piece on the board : moving it later will distort the wet slip pattern.

Check the slip consistency: it should coat the back of a spoon and drop off in a slow ribbon. Too thick, it will not flow when dragged. Too thin, the colours will bleed together rather than holding distinct veins. Stir in a few drops of water or a small pinch of dry clay powder to adjust.

Flood the surface with the base-colour slip using the mop brush. Work from one edge to the other in a single pass, keeping a wet edge so no dry ridges form. The layer should be approximately 2 mm deep across the whole surface.

While the base coat is still wet, load the liner brush with the contrasting slip. Trail it across the surface in parallel lines spaced about 15 mm apart, or dot it in blobs at irregular intervals for a different pattern.

Drag the needle tool or a comb in smooth, unhurried passes at 90 degrees to the contrasting lines. Each drag pulls the contrasting colour into the base, forming feathered veins. For a tighter marble, make alternating passes: left-to-right, then right-to-left, 10 mm apart.

Set the board on a level surface where it will not be disturbed. Leave untouched until the slip is no longer shiny : approximately 30 to 60 minutes depending on room temperature. Do not try to move or trim the piece before this stage or the slip will smear.
Once the slip has lost its sheen, re-cut the edges cleanly with the fettling knife if the slip has crept over the border. Leave to dry fully for 24 to 2 days before sealing with undiluted PVA.
What it looks like when it works

A successful marbled surface has continuous, unbroken veins that curve and taper like natural stone. The two colours remain distinct at the edges of each vein rather than merging into a single muddy tone. The base layer shows through between the veins as an even field of colour. There are no ridges, craters, or dry flakes lifting at the edges.
Common mistakes

Slip too thick: the contrasting lines sit on top of the base rather than sinking in, and the needle tears grooves rather than drawing the colour. Thin the slip a little at a time and test on a scrap piece.
Slip too thin: both colours bleed together on contact and the drag has no effect. Add a small amount of dry clay and stir until the spoon-coat test shows a slow ribbon.
Dragging too quickly: the needle skips over the wet surface and the veins break into dots. Slow the stroke to a steady, deliberate pace : about the speed of drawing a line with a pen.

Slip applied to bone-dry clay: the base coat beads and flakes away as it dries because there is no moisture in the clay body to allow adhesion. Dampen the surface first with a barely-damp sponge, or apply slip at leather-hard.
Moving the board too soon: the pattern shifts and the veins smear. Leave the piece completely undisturbed until the slip surface is dull and no longer wet to the touch.
